name: WordPress MCP Server
what_it_does: WordPress MCP server enabling AI agents to interact with WordPress sites — creating, reading, updating, and deleting posts and pages, managing media, handling comments, accessing user and taxonomy data, and integrating WordPress content management into agent-driven content creation and publishing workflows.
use_cases: Drafting and publishing blog posts from AI content creation agents
Managing WordPress media library from content management agents
Automating content updates and revisions from editorial workflow agents
Retrieving and analyzing post performance from analytics agents
Managing WordPress comments and moderation from community agents
Bulk content operations across WordPress sites from automation agents
not_for: Non-WordPress CMS platforms (Drupal, Ghost, Contentful require different MCPs)
WordPress.com free tier (requires Business plan or self-hosted for API access)
Teams without WordPress REST API or Application Password configured
best_when: An agent needs to create or manage content on a WordPress site — for AI-assisted blogging, automated content pipelines, or WordPress site administration.
avoid_when: You use a different CMS platform, or your WordPress site doesn't have REST API access enabled.

auth.notes: WordPress Application Passwords (recommended for self-hosted) or OAuth2 via WordPress.com for hosted. Application Passwords available in WordPress 5.6+. Generate from WordPress admin under user profile.

pricing.free_tier_limits: WordPress.org: free software; hosting costs vary
pricing.paid_tiers: WordPress.com: Business plan required for REST API access
pricing.notes: WordPress software is free and open source. Self-hosted WordPress has full API access. WordPress.com requires Business plan ($25/month) for REST API. MCP server is free open source from Automattic.

performance.rate_limits: Self-hosted: server capacity; WordPress.com: rate limits apply per plan

known_agent_gotchas: Application Password must be generated per-user in WordPress admin dashboard
WordPress REST API must be enabled (enabled by default in WP 5.6+)
Permalink structure must be enabled (non-plain) for REST API to work properly
WordPress.com vs self-hosted have different authentication flows
Published posts are immediately public — use draft status for staged content
Automattic-maintained MCP — higher quality than average community MCP
agent_readiness.security_notes: Application Password auth. HTTPS strongly recommended. Store credentials in env var. Official Automattic MCP. Limit application password scope. Use editor role user for content operations, not admin.

security_score_components.security_notes: Application Password auth. HTTPS required. Official Automattic MCP. Use editor user not admin. Store password in env var.
